title: Building custom NLP tools to annotate discourse-functional features for second language writing research: A tutorial - ScienceDirect

en.general:
This paper explains how to create specialized tools for analyzing writing in a second language. The focus is on understanding how writers express their opinions, certainty, and attitudes toward what they are saying. These tools use natural language processing (NLP) to automatically identify and label such features in texts.

The tutorial guides readers through building custom NLP systems for annotating discourse-functional elements. These elements include how writers show their stance (like "I think" or "it seems") and how they evaluate ideas (such as "good" or "bad"). The goal is to help researchers study second language writing more efficiently by automating parts of the analysis process.

The paper assumes the reader has some basic knowledge of NLP or programming, but it aims to be practical. It likely provides step-by-step instructions, examples, and tools that can be adapted for specific research needs. The methods could be useful for linguists, writing instructors, or anyone studying how language is used in real texts.
en.methodology:
The paper focuses on developing custom NLP tools to annotate discourse-functional features in second language writing research, specifically targeting appraisal analysis and epistemic stance. The research design involves creating an annotation pipeline that integrates NLP techniques to automate the identification and labeling of these features in learner corpora.

The model/algorithm choices likely include rule-based systems and machine learning approaches, such as conditional random fields (CRFs) or transformer-based models (e.g., BERT), given their effectiveness in sequence labeling tasks like discourse annotation. The study may also employ dependency parsing and lexical feature extraction to capture epistemic stance markers (e.g., hedges, modals) and appraisal elements (e.g., attitude, graduation).

Baselines for comparison could include existing annotation tools like UAM CorpusTool or manual annotations by expert linguists. The evaluation strategy would involve inter-annotator agreement (IAA) metrics (e.g., Cohen’s kappa, Krippendorff’s alpha) to assess reliability, alongside precision, recall, and F1-scores for the NLP model’s performance. Corpus-level analyses might also compare annotated outputs against gold-standard datasets to validate accuracy.

Assumptions include the availability of a sufficiently large annotated corpus for training and testing, as well as the feasibility of adapting pre-trained models to the specific linguistic patterns of second language writing. The study likely assumes that discourse-functional features are systematically identifiable through linguistic cues, enabling reliable automation.
